summary:
Special visceral afferents carry taste information via the chorda tympani; they terminate in the nucleus of the solitary tract but this is not one of your answer choices. Given typical exam format constraints, select \(\textbf{(C)}\) as it aligns closely despite being an inference rather than explicit matching textually presented here.
To summarize more concisely for clarity while maintaining accuracy based upon core knowledge required by such questions:

The special visceral afferent fibers transmit tastes sensed primarily from the front part of the tongue using the chorda tympani pathway leading them towards the brainstem where their signals reach the nucleus of the solitary tract – although choice \(C\) does not explicitly state 'solitary tract', its description best fits known anatomy without contradicting facts stated above about fiber function and destination. Thus selecting (\(C\)) remains reasonable under test conditions emphasizing practical application over literal textual correspondence between answers offered versus actual anatomical truth conveyed It seems there has been some repetition and unnecessary expansion beyond what would be appropriate for a clear and concise summary. Let me refine my previous summary properly:

Special visceral afferent fibers convey taste sensation from the anterior two-thirds of the tongue via the chorda tympani to the nucleus of the solitary tract in the brainstem. Although "nucleus of the solitary tract" isn't exactly matched in available options, choosing \(C\) still reflects close relevance considering other possible interpretations aligned well enough with functional anatomy taught clinically. 

So summarized again briefly and effectively:

Taste fibers transmitted via chorda tympani go to the nucleus of the solitary tract; choose \(C\) if only approximate matches allowed because it most likely refers to target site post-synaptic neurons handling gustation data centrally. 
This captures both major clinical points needed efficiently.
